data.title: StepStone outlines 37% core FRE margin target while expanding private wealth platform to $8B
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: StepStone Group Inc. (STEP) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Scott Hart highlighted record quarterly and annual results, stating "Our fee related earnings, FRE margin and adjusted net income per share were all at our highest levels ever for both our quarterly and annual results." He reported over $31 billion in assets raised during the year and $27.5 billion of growth in fee earning AUM, with a 29% organic growth rate in fiscal 2025, marking the company's best 12-month period since going public.
  • Hart emphasized expansion in private wealth, noting assets grew from $3.4 billion to over $8 billion year-over-year, driven by new products and expanded distribution. He added, "We expanded our distribution partners from roughly 300 unique platforms a year ago to almost 500 platforms today, and we continue to expand our offerings outside the U.S."
  • The CEO described strong fundraising momentum, with total gross inflows of $9.9 billion in the quarter, including $1.2 billion of evergreen subscriptions—the best private wealth quarter to date. Fee earning AUM reached over $121 billion, up $7.2 billion from the prior quarter.
  • Hart noted changing market conditions: "The backdrop obviously shifted in April and has seemingly shifted back in May with rapidly evolving global trade policy driving volatility in the public markets and creating widening bid ask spreads in the private markets." He added, "We expect that we will continue to operate in an environment characterized by uncertainty."
  • Mike McCabe, Head of Strategy, detailed over $31 billion gross AUM inflows for the year, with managed accounts and commingled funds contributing $21 billion and $10 billion, respectively. Notable fund closes included $1.2 billion for the real estate secondaries fund and $300 million for the growth equity fund. McCabe reported that the real estate secondaries fund finished at over $3.75 billion, "the largest real estate secondaries fund ever raised in the industry."
  • President and Co-COO Jason Ment discussed private wealth strategy, stating, "We now offer evergreen funds across credit, infrastructure, venture and growth equity, and of course our all private markets SPRIM Fund."
  • CFO David Park stated, "We earned fee revenues of $215 million, up 40% from the prior year quarter." Park added, "Fee related earnings were $94 million, up 85% from a year ago. FRE margin was 44% for the quarter, up more than 1,000 basis points versus the prior year quarter."

Outlook

  • Park said, "I think that 37% margin is a fair expectation as a starting point. But as you know, from quarter-to-quarter, the margins are going to vary, particularly the next quarter our merit increases took effect April 1. So you should see a slight bump up in compensation as well as incremental hirings."
  • Hart noted, "We are actually feeling fairly positive in terms of the pipeline of new opportunities, whether through RFPs that we are in the process of responding to...I would say that we continue to be pleased with the amount of opportunity that lies ahead of us."

Financial Results

  • StepStone reported a GAAP net loss attributable to the company of $18.5 million or ($0.24) per share. Fee related earnings reached $94.1 million, with an FRE margin of 44%. Retroactive fees contributed $15.7 million to fee revenues. Adjusted net income for the quarter was $80.6 million or $0.68 per share.
  • For the full year, dividends declared totaled $1.36 per share, up from $0.99 last year. The quarter included a base quarterly dividend of $0.24 and a supplemental dividend of $0.40, both payable June 30.
  • Net accrued carry finished the quarter at $738 million, down 1% from last quarter but up 16% over 12 months. The quarter also saw gross realized performance fees of $81 million and net realized performance fees of $42 million, both record levels.

Q&A

  • Ken Worthington, JPMorgan: Asked about one-time fees and margin expectations. Park replied, the quarter included about $4 million of one-time advisory fees and, after adjustments, "the FRE margin for the quarter would have been 37% versus the 40% just purely excluding retroactive fees."
  • Worthington followed up on pipeline visibility. Hart responded, "We are actually feeling fairly positive in terms of the pipeline of new opportunities...we probably don't have the same size of re-up opportunities coming, but still a very healthy re-up pipeline and expect to continue to execute on that at a very high re-up rate."
  • Ben Budish, Barclays: Asked about fundraising cadence and fee rates. Hart agreed with the "barbell" fundraising pattern and noted, "In terms of the flagship funds that are currently in market today, we've mentioned in the past that our private equity co-investment fund is back in market, have not had a first closing there yet."
  • Budish also inquired about fee rates. Park answered, "If you strip out the retroactive fees, it was about 94 basis points for the quarter. But again, that is a little bit elevated, I think, because you have to factor in timing of the close."
  • Michael Cyprys, Morgan Stanley: Asked about the secondaries marketplace and scaling. Hart said, "Do expect to see increased selling in the secondaries market...we are actively evaluating opportunities in this market," but "now clearly expect that to slow down a bit just given bid ad spread and likely delay in deals given the uncertainty in the market."
  • Cyprys also asked about private wealth platform evolution. Ment said, "The U.S. market continues to be a strong one for us and the European market continues to be a lot of white space as we continue to build-out a sales force as well as syndicate partners there."
  • Chris Kotowski, Oppenheimer: Inquired about the NCI buyout. McCabe stated, "We also issued $161 million of equity. So that was 3.2 million issued shares."
  • Unidentified Analyst, Goldman Sachs: Asked about retail distribution channels and competition. Ment said, "our allocation amongst the different channels is actually fairly consistent period-over-period...despite that competition, we've posted a couple of quarters in a row of best quarters ever."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on margin sustainability, fundraising outlook, and evolving fee structures, with a generally neutral to slightly positive tone, seeking clarity on recurring earnings and growth pipeline.
  • Management's tone was confident during prepared remarks and constructive in Q&A, emphasizing growth and operational strength. Park's comments included, "I think that 37% margin is a fair expectation as a starting point," showing measured optimism.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management was more assertive about record performance, while analysts maintained a consistent approach, probing for details on margin trends and fundraising cadence.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Fee related earnings increased from $74.1 million in Q3 to $94.1 million in Q4, with the FRE margin rising from 39% to 44% (37% core FRE margin, normalized for one-time and retroactive fees).
  • Private wealth AUM rose from $6 billion at the end of Q3 to over $8 billion in Q4, supported by expanded distribution.
  • Management reiterated a strong fundraising pipeline, while noting increased market volatility and scenario planning for asset allocation.
  • Analysts continued to focus on margin normalization, fee rate trends, and the sustainability of fundraising flows, echoing themes from the prior quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited "rapidly evolving global trade policy driving volatility in the public markets and creating widening bid ask spreads in the private markets."
  • The outlook highlighted ongoing uncertainty, with Hart stating the company will focus on "scenario planning to quickly and dynamically assess the impact by asset class, strategy, region and sector."
  • Analysts raised concerns about sustainability of margins after normalization for one-time items, as well as visibility and timing of fundraising and deployment, especially amid market volatility.

Final Takeaway

StepStone delivered record quarterly and annual results, driven by robust asset growth, exceptional fundraising, and expansion of the private wealth platform. The company achieved its highest-ever fee related earnings and margins, while setting expectations for a normalized core FRE margin around 37% going forward. Management remains optimistic about its fundraising pipeline and the opportunities ahead, while acknowledging increased market volatility and a focus on disciplined scenario planning to navigate the current environment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Oil prices extend losses as OPEC+ said to assess another production hike
data.description:
Crude oil prices settled lower for a third straight session on Thursday, as the possibility of another large production increase by OPEC+ for July added to concerns that global supply could outpace demand growth.

Another increase of 411K bbl/day for July is among the options under discussion ahead of the group's planned meeting on June 1, although no final agreement has been reached, Bloomberg reported, citing delegates.

A substantial production increase could exacerbate concerns of an oversupplied market, particularly as uncertainties surrounding trade talks and the broader economic outlook continue to cloud sentiment.

The speculation follows a report this week that oil production by OPEC member Kazakhstan has increased 2% this month, as the country continues to defy pressure from the cartel on reducing its output; Kazakhstan's oil production fell 3% in April, although still exceeding its OPEC+ quota.

OPEC likely made its decision to speed up production increases in part to punish members that were not complying with curbs, and could contribute to a continuation of the policy.

Geopolitics "remain pivotal for the next move in oil up to $70 Brent, or down to the $50s," Citi analysts wrote, pointing to "highly binary geopolitical risks" around U.S. negotiations on Iran's nuclear program and the Russia-Ukraine war.

Citi sees OPEC+ holding output at June levels through this year's H2, "but another three-month slug could put pressure back on the market," but meanwhile, the bank maintained its three-month Brent crude forecast at $60/bbl.

Oil prices fell in the previous session after data showed U.S. crude and fuel inventories showed surprise stock builds last week as crude imports hit a six-week high and gasoline and distillate demand slipped.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for July delivery finished -0.6% to $61.20/bbl, and front-month Brent crude (CO1:COM) for July delivery ended -0.7% to $64.44/bbl, the lowest settlement for both benchmarks since May 9.

Meanwhile, front-month June Nymex natural gas (NG1:COM) closed -3.4% at $3.253/MMBtu, as a large increase in underground storage - the biggest so far this injection season - confirms still soft seasonal demand; while not a surprise, the inventory build added some downward pressure to forward pricing, analysts said.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG)

data.title: Deckers outlines $150M tariff headwind and signals gross margin decline for fiscal 2026 as HOKA and UGG drive global growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Deckers Outdoor Corporation (DECK) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Stefano Caroti congratulated the team on "another exceptional year," highlighting full-year revenue growth of 16% to just under $5 billion, gross margin expansion to 57.9%, operating margin improvement to 23.6%, and EPS increasing 30% to $6.33. He credited the long-term strategy and "focus on the long term" as a "winning strategy, driving incredible progress over the past five years."
  • Caroti emphasized HOKA's global revenue increase of 24% to $2.2 billion and noted, "International revenue expanding 39% and now representing 34% of global revenue, up from 30% last year."
  • UGG delivered a 13% revenue increase to $2.5 billion, with "wholesale revenue increasing 15%" and "international revenue expanding 20%, now representing 39% of global sales."
  • Caroti announced Cindy Davis as the new Board chair, succeeding Mike Devine.
  • CFO Steve Fasching stated, "We exited fiscal year 2025 in a position of outstanding financial strength as our probability profile and rock solid balance sheet reinforce our ability to contesting behind our long-term strategies."

Outlook

  • Deckers is not providing formal guidance for fiscal year 2026 due to "macroeconomic uncertainty related to global trade policy," but shared that "we were targeting our brands to deliver another year of double-digit growth, led by mid-teen growth from HOKA and mid-single-digit growth from UGG" prior to new tariff uncertainty.
  • Fasching cited an expected "increase of up to $150 million to our cost of goods sold in fiscal year 2026, with the related yet to be determined impact to demand."
  • He noted, "We expect to absorb a portion of the tariff impact as we do not anticipate that these actions will fully offset incremental costs in fiscal year 2026."
  • For Q1 fiscal 2026, revenue is expected in the range of $890 million to $910 million, and diluted EPS in the range of $0.62 to $0.67 as compared to last year's $0.75.
  • Gross margin for Q1 is "expected to be down approximately 250 basis points versus last year due to higher freight costs expensed relative to last year increased promotional activity... and channel mix headwinds."

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter revenue was $1.02 billion, increasing 6% year-over-year, with HOKA up 10% and UGG up 4%.
  • Gross margin in Q4 was 56.7%, a 50 basis point increase from the prior year.
  • Fourth quarter diluted EPS was $1, up from $0.82 in the prior year period.
  • Full-year results included $4.986 billion in revenue, gross margin of 57.9%, SG&A spend of $1.71 billion, operating margin of 23.6%, and a full-year effective tax rate of 22.3%.
  • Deckers ended the year with $1.9 billion in cash and equivalents, repurchasing $567 million worth of shares in fiscal 2025.
  • The Board of Directors approved an increase of $2.25 billion to the company's stock repurchase authorization, bringing the total to $2.5 billion.

Q&A

  • Jonathan Komp, Baird: Asked about the slowdown in HOKA U.S. DTC and confidence in category dynamics. Fasching explained, "It was isolated in terms of some of the pressure that we saw in DTC in the U.S... we are encouraged by the online retention metrics from repeat consumers."
  • Komp also inquired about HOKA's growth trajectory. Fasching responded, "We do see this in a normalized environment in terms of a mid-teen grower," but noted macroeconomic noise and model changeovers impacting near-term results.
  • Jay Sole, UBS: Questioned the $150 million in tariff costs. Fasching clarified, "the $150 million is a gross number... we would look to recapture maybe up to half."
  • Laurent Vasilescu, BNP Paribas: Sought color on HOKA's DTC/wholesale growth and international mix. Caroti indicated strong pipeline and clean inventory, stating "Bondi 8, are pretty much liquidated in the marketplace."
  • Samuel Poser, Williams Trading: Asked about inventory strategy amid tariff risk. Fasching said, "you are going to see a bigger increase in inventory... bringing inventory in a little bit earlier in the U.S."
  • John Kernan, TD Cowen: Asked about UGG performance and wholesale expansion. Caroti stated, "UGG has a more solid base than it's ever had," and Fasching added, "demand for UGG remains incredibly strong."
  • Rick Patel, Raymond James Financial: Queried HOKA wholesale strategy. Caroti explained, "Our long-term aim is to have 50-50 split between DTC and wholesale."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts demonstrated a neutral to slightly cautious tone, frequently probing tariff impact, inventory management, and growth sustainability. Several questions revisited guidance caution and category momentum.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing brand strength and long-term opportunity but showed more measured optimism in Q&A, citing "noise going on in the U.S. economy" and uncertainty around demand following price adjustments. Caroti expressed, "I personally never felt stronger about the power of this brand."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone shifted from celebratory and growth-focused to more vigilant and contingency-oriented, with greater emphasis on uncertainty and mitigation strategies.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • In Q3, management provided formal fiscal-year guidance and raised growth outlook, while Q4 withdrew formal guidance for fiscal 2026 due to new tariff risks.
  • Previous quarter highlighted record gross margin and robust DTC growth; current quarter noted DTC softness in HOKA U.S. and anticipated gross margin contraction.
  • Analysts in Q3 focused on expansion and product launches, while Q4 questions centered on managing tariff headwinds, inventory, and wholesale expansion.
  • Management confidence remained strong in brand fundamentals but was more guarded in discussing near-term financial impacts and macro uncertainty.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged "macroeconomic uncertainty related to global trade policy" and expects a cost of goods sold increase of up to $150 million for fiscal 2026.
  • Fasching stated, "we expect to absorb a portion of the tariff impact as we do not anticipate that these actions will fully offset incremental costs."
  • Gross margin headwinds identified include tariffs, elevated promotion, upgraded material costs, and higher ocean freight rates.
  • Possible "demand erosion associated with the combination of price increases and general softness in the consumer spending environment" was cited.
  • Analysts repeatedly raised concerns about the magnitude of tariff impact and company mitigation strategies.

Final Takeaway

Deckers closed fiscal 2025 with record growth in revenue, margins, and earnings, propelled by the continued global momentum of HOKA and UGG. However, the company faces a new set of challenges in fiscal 2026, primarily from anticipated tariff-related cost increases and an uncertain macro environment. While management remains committed to long-term growth and brand investment, they acknowledge that gross margin and earnings will be pressured by up to $150 million in incremental costs, with partial mitigation through pricing and cost sharing. The strategic emphasis remains on brand strength, selective distribution expansion, and capital return, but the year ahead will test the company's ability to navigate shifting trade policy and consumer dynamics.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Intuit raises fiscal 2025 revenue growth guidance to 15% amid strong TurboTax Live and mid-market momentum
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Intuit Inc. (INTU) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Sasan Goodarzi stated, "We delivered an exceptional quarter with revenue growth of 15%, driven by outstanding performance across our platform. As a result, we are raising guidance across all total company metrics, including revenue, operating income, operating margin, and earnings per share." Goodarzi highlighted the impact of the company’s AI-driven expert platform, particularly in automating tasks and offering predictive insights for customers.
  • Goodarzi emphasized "outstanding tax results," expecting consumer group revenue to grow 10% for the year, with TurboTax Live customers projected to grow 24% and TurboTax Live revenue to increase 47%. He noted, "Our done-for-you experiences drove a 12% reduction in the average time a customer spent on their return with more than half of our do-it-yourself and do-it-with-me customers completing their return in under one hour."
  • Goodarzi also announced a "refreshed end-to-end platform that completes key jobs all in one place with a virtual team of AI agents doing much of the work side by side with our customers," with new product lineups and pricing to be introduced in the coming weeks. Progress in the mid-market was underscored by recent wins, including an 18-entity title company returning to Intuit after migrating to a competitor, driven by the value of the Intuit Enterprise Suite.
  • Goodarzi detailed management changes: "Marianna Tessel will lead small-business, Ashley Still will lead mid-market, and David Hahn will lead services consisting of money and workforce solutions." These leaders will report directly to the CEO to accelerate focus on growth opportunities.
  • CFO Sandeep Aujla reported, "Our third quarter results include revenue of $7.8 billion, up 15%; GAAP operating income of $3.7 billion, up 20%; non-GAAP operating income of $4.3 billion, up 17%; GAAP diluted earnings per share of $10.02, up 19% and non-GAAP diluted earnings per share of $11.65, up 18%."

Outlook

  • Aujla announced, "We are increasing our fiscal 2025 total revenue, operating income, implied operating margin, and earnings per share guidance. This includes: the revenue growth of 15%, up from prior guidance of 12% to 13%." GAAP operating income is now expected to grow 35%, non-GAAP operating income 18%, GAAP-diluted earnings per share 26% to 27%, and non-GAAP diluted earnings per share 18% to 19% for fiscal 2025.
  • For Q4 2025, guidance includes total company revenue growth of 17% to 18%, GAAP EPS of $0.84 to $0.89, and non-GAAP EPS of $2.63 to $2.68.
  • Aujla stated, "We continue to leverage AI to operate more efficiently and increase productivity across the company."

Financial Results

  • Consumer Group revenue reached $4 billion, up 11% in Q3. TurboTax Live revenue is expected to grow 47%, with customers growing 24%. The company expects total online paying units to grow 6% for the year, with a 13% increase in average revenue per return.
  • Global Business Solutions Group revenue grew 19%, driven by Online Ecosystem revenue growth of 20% (24% excluding Mailchimp). QuickBooks Online Accounting revenue rose 21%. Online payment volume grew 18%.
  • Credit Karma revenue grew 31% in Q3, with strength in credit cards, personal loans, and auto insurance. Fiscal 2025 Credit Karma revenue growth guidance was raised to 28%, up from 5% to 8%.
  • The company ended the quarter with $6.2 billion in cash and investments and $6.4 billion in debt. $754 million of stock was repurchased during the quarter.

Q&A

  • Siti Panigrahi, Mizuho: Asked about the sustainability of new growth in the assisted category. Goodarzi responded, "I feel very good about our progress. It was truly a breakthrough adoption year... there's still so much that we can do to make the end-to-end experience seamless."
  • Brad Zelnick, Deutsche Bank: Inquired about the drivers behind 47% Live growth. Goodarzi attributed growth to adoption within the base, breakthrough adoption of new full-service customers, and improvements in go-to-market, but noted areas for improvement in the onboarding experience for previous assisted customers.
  • Alex Zukin, Wolfe Research: Asked about AI agent launches and pricing. Goodarzi described new AI agents across business functions and a pricing strategy involving both value-based pricing and additional module charges. Aujla added that AI-driven efficiencies are expected to allow operating income growth to outpace revenue.
  • Raimo Lenschow, Barclays: Questioned Credit Karma’s visibility. Aujla cited execution, AI integration, and new product innovations as key drivers.
  • Kash Rangan, Goldman Sachs: Asked about pricing power and resilience. Goodarzi explained that over 90% of revenue is subscription-based and described Intuit as a "price disruptor" in the market.
  • Multiple analysts asked about mid-market strategy, AI monetization, and durability of TurboTax growth, with Goodarzi emphasizing early days in mid-market and AI agent rollout, and confidence in continued growth due to ongoing innovation.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed positive sentiment, focusing on outperformance in tax, AI innovation, and Credit Karma’s rebound. Questions were inquisitive, seeking sustainability of growth, details on new AI offerings, and pricing strategies.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone throughout, frequently using phrases like “we are confident,” “exceptional momentum,” and “we have a long runway ahead.” In Q&A, Goodarzi acknowledged areas for improvement but reiterated strong conviction in the company’s strategy and future.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management showed increased optimism, supported by higher guidance and stronger-than-expected results.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw raised guidance across revenue, operating income, and EPS, compared to reiterated guidance in Q2. TurboTax Live and Credit Karma growth rates accelerated, with Credit Karma guidance increased from 5-8% to 28% for the year.
  • Strategic focus shifted further toward AI-powered automation, mid-market expansion, and leadership realignment. Management’s tone was more confident, reflecting substantial outperformance and momentum.
  • Analysts in Q3 focused more on the durability and sustainability of recent outperformance and the implications of expanded AI offerings, while the previous quarter’s questions centered more on early tax season performance and AI-driven efficiencies.

Risks and Concerns

  • Aujla noted "the uncertain macro-environment" but cited the mission-critical nature of Intuit’s offerings and data-driven insights as key mitigants.
  • Goodarzi acknowledged that "there was still a lot of friction" for previous assisted customers, identifying onboarding experience as a focus area.
  • The company recognized Mailchimp as a headwind, noting that improved outcomes will take several quarters, but stated that performance across other business lines remains strong.

Final Takeaway

Intuit’s management highlighted exceptional third-quarter results supported by double-digit growth in key business lines, robust adoption of AI-driven solutions, and significant momentum in both consumer and business segments. Raised guidance for fiscal 2025 underscores management’s confidence in sustained growth, ongoing margin expansion, and continued disruption across the assisted tax and mid-market categories, positioning the company for further scale and innovation in the coming quarters.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Workday reiterates $8.8B subscription revenue target for FY 2026 as AI adoption surges
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Workday, Inc. (WDAY) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Carl Eschenbach reported "Workday delivered a solid first quarter. We drove 13% subscription revenue growth and a non-GAAP operating margin of 30%." Eschenbach highlighted strong adoption across key verticals, geographies, and customer segments, with particular momentum in AI products, stating, "New ACV across our AI products more than doubled year-over-year in Q1. And roughly 25% of our customer expansions in the quarter included one or more of these products."
  • Eschenbach detailed new customer relationships with United Airlines, Pilot Travel Centers, and Mutual of Omaha Insurance Company, and noted expansions with FedEx, CVS Health, ASDA stores, and Chipotle. He revealed "more than 30% of our net new wins this quarter were once again full suite, and in our focus industries of SLED and healthcare it was more than 50%."
  • Eschenbach announced the launch of WorkdayGO to target the emerging and medium enterprise market, which "helped our emerging and medium enterprise teams deliver a strong quarter."
  • Five industries now exceed $1 billion in annual recurring revenue, with Manufacturing and Tech and Media joining Financial Services, Retail and Hospitality, and Professional and Business Services.
  • Eschenbach highlighted ongoing investments in AI with the appointment of Peter Bailis as the new Chief Technology Officer to "lead our AI and ML initiatives, driving our vision forward."
  • CFO Zane Rowe stated, "Our solid Q1 results highlight the ongoing progress across our strategic growth areas, and the continued efficiencies we are driving throughout the business."

Outlook

  • Workday reiterated its full-year subscription revenue guidance of $8.8 billion. Rowe explained, "Our Q1 subscription revenue performance, and progress across the key initiatives Carl highlighted earlier, positions us well for the year and we are reiterating our subscription revenue guidance of $8.8 billion."
  • Q2 FY '26 subscription revenue is anticipated to be approximately $2.16 billion, "growth of 13%." Rowe added, "We continue to expect a slightly faster pace of subscription revenue growth in the second half of the year, particularly in Q4."
  • Rowe maintained guidance for FY '26 professional services revenue of approximately $700 million and FY '26 non-GAAP operating margin of approximately 28.5%.
  • The company expects FY '26 operating cash flow of $2.75 billion and capital expenditures of approximately $250 million.

Financial Results

  • Subscription revenue in Q1 was $2.059 billion, up 13%; professional services revenue reached $181 million; total revenue was $2.24 billion, also up 13%.
  • U.S. revenue totaled $1.68 billion, and international revenue was $559 million.
  • 12-month subscription revenue backlog (cRPO) was $7.63 billion at the end of Q1, increasing 15.6%.
  • Total subscription revenue backlog at quarter end was $24.62 billion, up 19%. Gross revenue retention rates remained at 98%.
  • Non-GAAP operating income for the quarter was $677 million, representing a non-GAAP operating margin of 30.2%. GAAP operating income was $39 million, impacted by a $166 million restructuring charge.
  • Q1 operating cash flow was $457 million, up 23%. $293 million of shares were repurchased during the quarter, with $509 million in remaining authorization as of April 30. The Board approved a new $1 billion buyback authorization. Workday ended the quarter with $8 billion in cash and marketable securities.

Q&A

  • Kirk Materne, Evercore ISI: Asked about macro conditions and confidence behind reiterating guidance. Eschenbach responded, "We continue to see progress across many of our key initiatives and investment areas, whether it's AI...or how we're pushing Financials deeper into the market...All of that comes together to help us feel confident to reiterate the guide of $8.8 billion for the rest of the year."
  • Brad Sills, Bank of America: Inquired about WorkdayGO's impact on medium enterprise. Eschenbach described accelerated deployments and specific targeting, "Deployments now between us and our partners can be done as little as sixty days, which is a significant change from what we've seen in the past."
  • Mark Murphy, JPMorgan: Sought insight on ExtendPro adoption and FINS growth. Eschenbach highlighted "ExtendPro SKU more than doubled year-over-year" and strong results from FINS sales efforts.
  • Brent Thill, Jefferies: Questioned hiring plans post-restructuring and European pipeline. Rowe said, "Our intention is to grow back. We're very thoughtful in those hires." Eschenbach added, "I will tell you our competitive win rates are very strong."
  • Michael Turrin, Wells Fargo: Asked about billings and capital allocation. Rowe explained, "Billings itself does vary on a quarter-to-quarter basis...We feel good about it."
  • Brad Zelnick, Deutsche Bank: Asked about AI expansion rates. Eschenbach stated, "The last three quarters, it's varied from 25% to 30% of our sales back to base included in AI SKU."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a slightly cautious to neutral tone, repeatedly probing about macro uncertainty, AI adoption timelines, and cost discipline, but acknowledged strong execution and margin performance.
  • Management demonstrated confidence in prepared remarks and during Q&A, frequently using phrases like "we continue to see progress" and "we feel good about the setup here for the year," particularly when addressing macro risks or reiterating guidance. The tone was consistent with the previous quarter, though slightly more focused on resilience amid uncertainty.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Subscription revenue growth moderated to 13% from 16% in the previous quarter.
  • Non-GAAP operating margin improved to 30.2% from 26.4%.
  • Guidance for subscription revenue ($8.8 billion) and margin (28.5%) was maintained, with continued focus on AI, partner ecosystem, and international markets.
  • Analysts in both calls focused on macro environment, AI monetization, and international market trends, with management providing similar reassurances but highlighting increased traction in AI and new initiatives like WorkdayGO in the current quarter.
  • The appointment of a new CTO for AI and ML marks a strategic shift from the previous quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Eschenbach cited ongoing macro uncertainty, particularly in SLED and international markets, "No company is immune to these challenges, and we're watching it across particular markets such as SLED and our international business."
  • Management is monitoring potential headwinds from government funding in higher education and healthcare sectors.
  • Rowe acknowledged, "While there is heightened macro uncertainty, particularly across certain markets and verticals, we haven't seen this meaningfully impact our business and our growth prospects."

Final Takeaway

Workday began fiscal 2026 with solid growth in subscription revenue and operating margin, fueled by accelerating AI adoption, notable customer wins, and strong partner contributions. The company reiterated its $8.8 billion subscription revenue target for the year, citing balanced performance across industries and geographies, continued product innovation, and strategic investments in AI and emerging markets as key drivers of confidence amid an uncertain macro environment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Copart signals robust operational investments and storage expansion amid active storm season and shifting insurance cycles
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Copart, Inc. (CPRT) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Jeff Liaw reported that "global insurance volume remained relatively flat year-over-year with a nominal decline of 0.3% globally in unit sales and 0.9% in the United States," but noted that "accounting for the extra business day of leap year 2024, global insurance and U.S. insurance units sold grew by 1.3% and 0.6%, respectively." Liaw emphasized that total loss frequency in the United States reached 22.8% in the first calendar quarter of 2025, up approximately 100 basis points from last year, driven by rising vehicle complexity and repair costs.
  • Liaw highlighted Copart’s preparation for an expected active 2025 storm season, referencing the acquisition of Hall Ranch in South Florida for nearly 400 usable acres of vehicle storage, which now enables Copart to “handle a storm more than 3 times the size of the largest Florida storms on record in Copart history.”
  • CFO Leah Stearns stated: “During the quarter, our global unit sales increased 1%, which reflects the modest headwind from the prior period being leap year. On a per business day basis, our global unit sales increased over 2%.” Stearns also described strong trends in non-insurance U.S. unit volume and a 14% year-over-year growth in Blue Car, which serves bank, rental, and fleet partners.

Outlook

  • Management indicated continued investments in physical storage, technology, and operational readiness for both storm response and daily business. Liaw explained that “our preparation is not an ad hoc spring event, but in fact a year-round exercise for us as a company.”
  • Stearns noted that secular trends in favor of rising total loss frequency are expected to drive long-term growth, while cyclical impacts associated with uninsured and underinsured motorists may influence near-term volumes.

Financial Results

  • Stearns reported that "global revenue increased to $1.2 billion." Global service revenue increased nearly $88 million or over 9% from the third quarter of 2024, and U.S. service revenue grew by 8%. International service revenue was up about 18%.
  • U.S. purchase vehicle revenue rose by about $20 million or 22%, while purchase vehicle gross profit decreased $13 million, or about 187%, which included a $12 million out-of-period adjustment related to prior quarters. International purchase vehicle revenue decreased over $23 million or 25%, but international purchase vehicle gross profit increased by over $2 million or about 22%.
  • Global gross profit was approximately $552 million, a $27 million increase, with a gross margin of 46%. U.S. gross profit was about $480 million with a 48% gross margin. International gross profit reached $73 million, with a 35% gross margin.
  • Third quarter GAAP operating income increased to approximately $452 million, and net income rose to $407 million or $0.42 per diluted share. Copart maintained liquidity of over $5.6 billion, including nearly $4.4 billion in cash.

Q&A

  • Bob Labick, CJS Securities, asked about the strategic value of Copart’s land assets for Blue Car and whole car customers. Jeff Liaw responded that physical storage is “essential to our value proposition for many sellers,” adding that “storage is increasingly difficult to procure anywhere in the United States, anywhere in any of the countries we serve.”
  • Labick inquired about the cyclical shift in insured motorists and its impact on volumes. Liaw stated, “We see cyclicality in it...around the time of the global financial crisis in 2008 and 2009 there was a local trough in insurance coverage. I think it’s not a surprise that we’re experiencing that again now.”
  • Craig Kennison, Baird, questioned Copart’s Purple Wave partnership and the effects of tariffs. Liaw explained that Purple Wave operates in an “uncertain environment, courtesy of the tariffs,” and described that tariffs have “created meaningful uncertainty for our clients, the insurance industry and otherwise.”
  • Chris Bottiglieri, BNP Paribas, asked about market share and auction price drivers. Liaw noted that “we’ve gained share...for many years,” attributing this to investments in land and technology. CFO Stearns added that the affordability gap in emerging markets continues to drive strong international buyer activity.
  • Jash Patwa, JPMorgan, asked about G&A spend trends and legislative impacts on storage fees and total loss thresholds. Stearns attributed G&A increases to salesforce investments at Purple Wave. Liaw discussed that legislation capping storage fees likely benefits insurance companies, while total loss threshold changes would not have a significant effect on Copart’s business model.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts displayed a neutral to slightly cautious tone, probing on asset utilization, market share dynamics, effects of tariffs, and G&A run rates, seeking clarity on business resilience amid macro uncertainty.
  • Management’s tone was confident and focused on long-term secular trends, with Liaw stating, “We are excited to continue to invest our time and resources in growing and enhancing our capabilities.” During Q&A, management responded in detail, acknowledging cyclical headwinds but reiterating operational strengths and strategic investments.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment remained consistent, with ongoing attention to macro and regulatory headwinds. Management maintained a steady and confident tone, emphasizing continued investment and operational readiness.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language remained consistent, with a continued focus on storage expansion, technology, and operational efficiency. Strategic focus slightly shifted towards readiness for an active storm season and navigating macro uncertainty in insurance coverage rates.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on macroeconomic cycles, G&A trends, and market share. In Q3, more attention was given to storm season preparedness and legislative developments.
  • Key metric changes include a modest increase in global unit sales and revenue, but a notable decrease in purchase vehicle gross profit due to adjustments. International gross margins improved despite revenue declines.
  • Management’s tone and confidence remained stable, emphasizing secular growth and operational investments. Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly cautious approach.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited softness in the heavy equipment auction space, driven by uncertainty over infrastructure spending and tariffs.
  • There was mention of lower assignments, faster cycle times, and reduced low-value unit-aged inventory as key factors in declining inventory. Management’s mitigation includes ongoing investment in operational efficiency and digital platforms.
  • Legislative risks around storage fee caps and total loss thresholds were discussed, but management downplayed their potential to materially impact Copart’s core business model.

Final Takeaway

Copart’s third quarter 2025 results showcase continued investment in operational capacity, technology, and storage, positioning the company for both day-to-day strength and heightened storm season activity. Although global insurance volumes were flat, secular trends in total loss frequency and international demand underpin a resilient outlook. Management remains confident in its strategy, citing operational readiness, strong liquidity, and ongoing investments as key drivers for long-term growth, while addressing cyclical headwinds and legislative uncertainties with clear mitigation strategies.

data.title: EPlus signals low single-digit net sales growth and mid-single-digit EBITDA increase amid services pivot and AI investments
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ePlus inc. (PLUS) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Mark Marron reported that "our financial results continue to reflect the ongoing industry wide shift towards ratable and subscription based revenue models," with higher gross profitability and margin expansion despite lower net sales and gross billings. Marron further highlighted, "operating metrics were strong with double digit increases in gross profit, operating income, adjusted EBITDA and diluted earnings per share."
  • Marron stated, "Q4 net sales declined 10.2% year-over-year, driven primarily by a reduction in product sales due in part to economic uncertainty as well as a tough compare to the prior year." He emphasized, "gross profit rose by nearly 12% and gross margin expanded 580 basis points year-over-year to 29.3% in the fourth quarter."
  • Services revenue continued to climb, with Marron citing a 33% increase for the quarter and 37% for the year. Managed services grew 16.6% for the quarter and 24.6% for the year.
  • The CEO underscored strategic investments in AI, cloud, security, and networking, and noted, "ePlus is the only NVIDIA partner in North America that has achieved both the NVIDIA DGX ready Super Pod specialization and DGX ready managed service provider specializations."
  • Security now represents 22% of gross billings on a trailing 12-month basis.
  • Marron described the year-end cash position as "a record cash position of approximately $389 million."
  • CFO Elaine Marion stated, "Consolidated net sales in the fiscal 2025 fourth quarter were $498.1 million, down from $554.5 million in the fourth quarter of fiscal 2024. And our consolidated adjusted EBITDA was $43.8 million, up from $36.8 million in the prior year, which exceeded our expectations as we captured increased gross profit from the sales of product."

Outlook

  • Marron introduced fiscal year 2026 guidance: "we are initiating fiscal year 2026 guidance for net sales growth of low single digits, while we expect gross profit and adjusted EBITDA to grow at mid-single digits over the prior fiscal year."
  • The guidance assumes some impact from economic uncertainty but does not factor in recessionary conditions or other unexpected developments.

Financial Results

  • Consolidated net sales for Q4 were $498.1 million.
  • Consolidated adjusted EBITDA for the fourth quarter was $43.8 million.
  • Technology business net sales declined 10.4% year-over-year to $487.2 million.
  • Service revenues increased 33% year-over-year, with professional services up 48% and managed services up 17%.
  • Fourth quarter consolidated gross profit increased 11.8% to $145.8 million, with gross margin rising to 29.3%.
  • Consolidated net earnings for the quarter rose 14.6% to $25.2 million or $0.95 per diluted share.
  • Full year consolidated net sales were $2.07 billion, down from $2.23 billion in fiscal 2024.
  • Full year consolidated gross profit rose 3.3% to $569.1 million, with gross margin expanding 270 basis points to 27.5%.
  • Cash position at the end of fiscal 2025 was $389.4 million.

Q&A

  • Greg Burns, Sidoti & Company, asked about the demand environment and product side softness. Marron responded, "We are seeing pickup in the data center, cloud and security space, that's not surprising... We did not see a pickup in networking year-over-year. So networking was down pretty big still for us."
  • Burns inquired about enterprise AI adoption and its impact on guidance. Marron answered, "Not yet, Greg, maybe towards the end of the year, beginning of next year... So we think a lot of that will be on the services side that will drive for us, which is a good thing, it's our most profitable business." He also noted, "We've made investments with the training of our sales teams. We also just got the NVIDIA Super Pod specialization, which is their high end computing. So over time, we think that infrastructure spend will pick up but it will take a little bit of time."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised concerns about ongoing product demand softness but showed interest in the growth areas of AI and managed services; the tone was neutral to slightly cautious.
  • Management’s prepared remarks conveyed confidence, especially highlighting margin expansion and the services pivot, while the Q&A responses were measured, acknowledging challenges in networking and product sales but expressing optimism for services and AI.
  • Compared with the previous quarter, management's tone appeared more confident about services-led growth, while analysts remained focused on demand softness and the timing of AI opportunity realization.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance shifted from a revenue range of $2.07 billion to $2.11 billion in Q3 to low single-digit net sales growth for FY26, with mid-single-digit gross profit and EBITDA growth expected.
  • Management’s confidence in the services pivot increased, with a larger emphasis on AI capabilities and NVIDIA partnerships.
  • Analysts continued to press on product demand and AI adoption timing, similar to the previous quarter’s focus on product sales softness and netted down revenues.
  • Key financial metrics improved in gross margin and adjusted EBITDA versus the prior quarter.
  • Strategic priorities shifted further towards high-growth technology areas, subscriptions, and managed services.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited economic uncertainty, tariffs, and government spending as factors impacting sales.
  • Continued softness in networking and product demand was acknowledged as a headwind.
  • The company expects ongoing impact from the industry shift to ratable and subscription-based revenue models.
  • Analyst questions highlighted the risk of delayed AI infrastructure investment and the lag in networking recovery.

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized that ePlus is capitalizing on the accelerating industry shift to services, subscriptions, and high-growth technology segments, delivering strong margin expansion and profitability despite headwinds in product sales. Strategic investments in AI, cloud, and security are expected to drive future growth, supported by a robust balance sheet and a disciplined approach to capital allocation. The company projects low single-digit net sales growth and mid-single-digit gains in gross profit and adjusted EBITDA for fiscal 2026, reflecting confidence in the ongoing transformation of its business model and its ability to adapt to evolving customer needs.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Gold price turns lower as dollar bounces higher, but debt concerns linger
data.description:
Gold futures turned lower Thursday after a three-day winning streak, as the dollar strengthened and investors locked in profits after prices touched a two-week high earlier in the session.

The U.S. dollar index rose 0.5%, rebounding after a steep slide since Moody's started the week by downgrading its U.S. credit rating, which exacerbated concerns over the U.S. debt burden and fiscal outlook, weighing on risk sentiment in recent days; a higher dollar makes bullion more expensive for foreign currency holders.

The U.S. bond market has been concerned about President Trump's "big beautiful" tax and spending bill, which finally passed the House of Representatives by a single vote and now moves on to the Senate; the legislation, as currently written, would add ~$3.8T to the federal government's $36.2B in debt over the next decade, according to the Congressional Budget Office, but includes an increase in the debt limit.

"We're seeing some profit taking pressure from recent gains and a firmer U.S. dollar index is another bearish factor," Kitco Metals analyst Jim Wycoff said in a note, but "the specter of a shaky global bond market is going to be a bullish underlying factor for the gold market that's going to limit the downside."

Meanwhile, traders continue to monitor developments in the Middle East, with another round of nuclear talks between the U.S. and Iran set to take place on Friday.

Front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for May delivery ended -0.5% to $3,292.30/oz, and front-month May Comex silver (XAGUSD:CUR) finished -1.2% to $33.047/oz, snapping three-session winning streaks for both precious metals.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:GLD), (NYSEARCA:GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)

data.title: Ross Stores withdraws annual guidance amid tariff uncertainty, projects Q2 EPS range of $1.40–$1.55
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Ross Stores (ROST) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO James Conroy emphasized the company’s resilience despite a volatile external environment, stating, "total sales grew 3% to $5 billion with comparable store sales flat versus last year. Earnings per share were $1.47 compared to $1.46 last year, while net income for the period was $479 million versus $488 million for the same period in 2024."
  • Conroy highlighted sequential monthly sales improvement, with cosmetics as the strongest merchandise area and the Southeast leading geographically. He noted, "our dd's DISCOUNTS brand continued its strong momentum from 2024 with another quarter of solid sales and operating profits."
  • Conroy addressed tariff impacts, saying the company will work to balance pricing and merchandise margin compression, adding, "it is possible that we will see short-term pressure on our profitability."
  • Inventory strategy was detailed with "total consolidated inventories were up 88% versus last year mainly due to opportunistic buys during the period. Average store inventories were up 4% in line with our plan and packaway merchandise represented 41% of total inventory."
  • Conroy announced the opening of 16 new Ross and 3 dd’s DISCOUNTS locations, continuing the plan for about 90 new stores this year, and stated, "there are simply too many unknown variables that are limiting our visibility into the second half of the fiscal year and we believe it is prudent to withdraw our previously provided annual guidance at this time."
  • CFO Adam Orvos reported, "first quarter operating margin of 12.2% was similar to last year. Cost of goods sold was relatively unchanged from a year ago. Merchandise margin declined 45 basis points, mainly due to higher ocean freight costs and the initial impact of tariffs."
  • Orvos added, "during the first quarter, we repurchased 2 million shares of common stock for an aggregate cost of $263 million under the company's two-year $2.1 billion authorization."

Outlook

  • Orvos provided Q2 guidance: "comparable store sales are projected to be flat to up 3%. Earnings per share for the second quarter are now projected to be in the range of $1.40 to $1.55 and includes a cost impact of $0.11 to $0.16 from the announced tariffs."
  • Total sales for Q2 are forecast to increase 2% to 6% versus the prior year, with operating margin projected between 10.7% and 11.4%, including a 90 to 120 basis point negative tariff impact.
  • Management withdrew full-year guidance, citing "too many unknown variables that are limiting our visibility into the second half of the fiscal year."

Financial Results

  • Sales and earnings performed at the high-end of company expectations for the quarter. Operating margin was flat at 12.2% year-over-year.
  • Merchandise margin was pressured by tariffs and ocean freight; occupancy and distribution costs rose modestly.
  • SG&A was flat year-over-year, as lower incentive compensation was offset by sales deleverage.
  • Inventory was notably higher due to opportunistic buying, with packaway merchandise steady as a share of inventory.
  • Share buyback activity continued, and the company remains on track for a $1.05 billion repurchase in 2025.

Q&A

  • Matthew Boss, JPMorgan: Asked about drivers of sequential comp improvement and May trends. Conroy responded, "The sequential improvement was really broad-based across the merchandise hierarchy... we did guide to a flat to a plus three. So that should give you some sense of how we feel about the health of the business." Hartshorn explained tariff mitigation strategies, including vendor negotiations, careful pricing, and leveraging closeouts and packaway inventory.
  • Lorraine Hutchinson, Bank of America: Inquired about the gross margin hit from tariffs and whether impact may decrease. Hartshorn said, "Second quarter impact really includes... orders that were already in place when the tariffs were announced... we'll have to wait and see how the macro economy and retail environment evolves and what the outlook looks for inflation."
  • Mark Altschwager, Baird: Asked about inventory risk and strategic initiatives. Conroy noted, "in the short-term, I think there'll be availability of closeouts... we think we can get through that with no problem."
  • Tracy Kogan, Citigroup: Queried about tariff mitigation and customer trade down. Hartshorn said, "In the second quarter, it does include some mitigation," and saw broad-based comps across income bands.
  • Michael Binetti, Evercore ISI: Requested guidance range rationale and sourcing flexibility. Hartshorn explained the broader range was "out of abundance of caution given the macroeconomic and geopolitical environments."
  • Alexandra Straton, Morgan Stanley: Asked about branded strategy and women's apparel. Conroy stated, "we feel very good about the team's execution of the branded strategy... we don't expect margin headwinds going forward any longer from the brand strategy." Ladies business was slightly better than chain average.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on tariff impacts, inventory management, and the company’s visibility, with a generally neutral to slightly cautious tone, pressing for specifics on mitigation and strategic flexibility.
  • Management maintained a cautious, pragmatic tone, often reiterating uncertainty and highlighting multiple mitigation levers. Conroy stated, "there are simply too many unknown variables that are limiting our visibility."
  • Compared to last quarter, the tone was more defensive, with management openly acknowledging lower visibility and the withdrawal of annual guidance as a prudent step.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance shifted from a previously provided annual outlook in Q4 to a withdrawal in Q1, signaling increased uncertainty due to tariffs and macroeconomic factors.
  • Management expanded discussion of tariff impacts and mitigation, which was only a minor topic last quarter.
  • Analysts’ focus moved from branded strategy and store productivity in Q4 to tariff risk, inventory strategy, and the flexibility of sourcing in Q1.
  • Management’s confidence in key initiatives, such as the branded strategy, remained, but overall tone reflected greater caution and less optimism regarding near-term trends.

Risks and Concerns

  • Elevated tariffs on Chinese imports, with management warning of "short-term pressure on our profitability."
  • Limited visibility on second-half performance due to consumer sentiment, inflation, and trade policy unpredictability.
  • Possible receipt risk from supply chain disruptions, though inventory is positioned to mitigate gaps.
  • Management cited broad-based inflationary pressure and acknowledged the potential for further impacts if macro conditions worsen.

Final Takeaway

Ross Stores delivered Q1 results at the high end of expectations despite a challenging environment, with improved sales momentum, solid inventory positioning, and strong execution of strategic initiatives such as the branded strategy. However, elevated tariff risk and macro uncertainty prompted the withdrawal of annual guidance, with management focusing on multiple mitigation levers and maintaining a cautious approach for the coming quarters. The outlook for Q2 projects comparable sales between flat and up 3%, and EPS in the $1.40 to $1.55 range, reflecting ongoing tariff impacts and a measured stance amid unpredictable trade and consumer conditions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Trump threatens to limit imports if countries don't pay more for drugs
data.description:
President Trump on Thursday threatened to limit imports of products from foreign countries if they don't work with pharmaceutical companies to pay more for drugs as a way to lower the costs for medicines in the United States.

Earlier this month, the president signed an Executive Order tying the prices of prescription drugs in the U.S. to what they cost in other countries under a program dubbed "Most Favored Nation."

If countries fight against drugmakers, "That's ok. We are not going to let you send any more cars into the United States," Trump said at a White House event for the release of the Make America Healthy Again report.

"Or we're not going to let you sell more wine or liquor or alcohol, or something that's actually much more important to them than the drugs. And we're going to be able to force that issue if we need to."

In his remarks, Trump predicted that Americans would save as much as 80% on prescription drugs compared to what they pay now with the Most Favored Nation program.

He also contended the MFN wouldn't impact pharmaceutical companies' bottom lines. "There shouldn't be a hit on their stock," adding that their income source will be "redistributed...so [other countries are] going to pay a little more, and we're going to pay a lot less."
